attempts.prompt:
You are helping a robotic planning task. 
    Given the image of a scene and an instruction, generate the PDDL file with objects, initial state, and goal specification.
    Your output should adhere to the constraints defined in the domain file.
    You must output the PDDL file in the correct format.
    Examples of PDDL problems given a different domain instruction:
    Domain: hanoi
    Instruction: Move all disks to the rightmost peg.
    PDDL problem:
    (define (problem hanoi3)
        (:domain hanoi)
        (:objects
            peg1
            peg2
            orange_disk1
            orange_disk2
        )
            (:init
                (clear orange_disk1)
                (on orange_disk1 orange_disk2)
                (on orange_disk2 peg1)
            )
        (:goal (and (on orange_disk2 peg2) (on orange_disk1 orange_disk2)))
    )
    
    For the current domain, this is the domain file:
    (define (domain put_task)
 (:requirements
  :adl
 )
 (:types
  agent
  microwave fridge sink - receptacle  ; an object is a receptacle if it can hold things
  knife receptacle - object           ; if not an receptacle, default to object
  )


 (:predicates
    (openable ?r - receptacle)                                ; true if a receptacle is openable
    (opened ?r - receptacle)                                  ; true if a receptacle is opened
    (inReceptacle ?o - object ?r - object)                ; object ?o is in receptacle ?r
    (holds ?a - agent ?o - object)                            ; object ?o is held by agent ?a
    (holdsAny ?a - agent)                                     ; agent ?a holds an object
    (isClean ?o - object)                                     ; true if the object has been clean in sink
    (isHot ?o - object)                                       ; true if the object has been heated up
    (isCool ?o - object)                                      ; true if the object has been cooled
    (isOn ?o - object)                                        ; true if the object is on
    (isToggled ?o - object)                                   ; true if the object has been toggled
    (isSliced ?o - object)                                    ; true if the object is sliced
    (atLocation ?a - agent ?o - object)
 )

;; agent goes to receptacle
 (:action GotoLocation
    :parameters (?a - agent ?lEnd - object)
    :precondition (and
            (not (atLocation ?a ?lEnd))
            )
    :effect (and
                (atLocation ?a ?lEnd)
                (forall (?l - object)
                    (when (and (atLocation ?a ?l) (not (= ?l ?lEnd)))
                        (not (atLocation ?a ?l))))
                (forall (?l - object)
                     (when (inReceptacle ?l ?lEnd) (atLocation ?a ?l)))
            )
 )

;; agent opens receptacle
 (:action OpenObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?r - receptacle)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (openable ?r)
            )
    :effect (and
                (opened ?r)
            )
 )
;; agent closes receptacle
 (:action CloseObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?r - receptacle)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (openable ?r)
            (opened ?r)
            )
    :effect (and
                (not (opened ?r))
            )

 )

;; agent picks up object
 (:action PickupObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?o - object)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?o)
            (not (holdsAny ?a))
            )
    :effect (and
                (holds ?a ?o)
                (holdsAny ?a)
                (forall (?r - receptacle)
                    (not (inReceptacle ?o ?r))
                )
            )
 )

;; agent puts down an object in a receptacle
 (:action PutObjectInReceptacle
    :parameters (?a - agent ?o - object ?r - receptacle)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (holds ?a ?o)
            )
    :effect (and
                (inReceptacle ?o ?r)
                (not (holds ?a ?o))
                (not (holdsAny ?a))
            )
 )

;; agent cleans some object
 (:action CleanObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?r - sink ?o - object)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (holds ?a ?o)
            )
    :effect (and
                (isClean ?o)
            )
 )


;; agent heats-up some object
 (:action HeatObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?r - microwave ?o - object)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (holds ?a ?o)
            )
    :effect (and
                (isHot ?o)
            )
 )

;; agent cools some object
 (:action CoolObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?r - fridge ?o - object)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?r)
            (holds ?a ?o)
            )
    :effect (and
                (isCool ?o)
            )
 )


;; agent toggle object
 (:action ToggleObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?o - object)
    :precondition (and
            (atLocation ?a ?o)
            )
    :effect (and
                (when (isOn ?o)
                    (not (isOn ?o)))
                (when (not (isOn ?o))
                    (isOn ?o))
                (isToggled ?o)
            )
 )


;; agent slices some object with a knife
 (:action SliceObject
    :parameters (?a - agent ?co - object ?ko - knife)
    :precondition
            (and
                (atLocation ?a ?co)
                (holds ?a ?ko)
            )
    :effect (and
                (isSliced ?co)
            )
 )


)
    
        The image of the scene has been provided.
        Please first analyze the image and then generate the PDDL problem.
        
    The robot is initially not at any object's location. It needs to first go to a location to interact with an object. If an object A can hold another object B, it is a receptacle and must be recognized as such.
    Instruction: Put a racket on a dresser.
    

SCENE CAPTION:
Scene Description:

The scene contains two primary areas: a wooden dresser and a bed with adjacent floor space.

1. Objects and their types:
   - The dresser is a large, wooden piece of furniture with multiple drawers. It is not defined as a receptacle in the domain, so it is treated as a generic object. It is positioned against a wall and serves as a surface for placing items.
   - A laptop is resting on the dresser. It is a grey, open laptop with a visible keyboard and screen. It is an object (not a receptacle).
   - A lamp with a white shade and a dark blue base is also on the dresser. It is an object.
   - A tennis racket with a red-orange frame and yellow strings is lying on the beige carpeted floor, partially under the bed. It is an object.
   - The bed has a dark blue blanket and two green square pillows. The bed frame is dark grey. The bed is not defined as a receptacle in the domain.
   - The floor is covered with a light beige carpet.

2. Quantities of each object type:
   - Receptacles: 0 (none of the objects in the scene are defined as receptacles in the domain: microwave, fridge, sink)
   - Objects: 5 (dresser, laptop, lamp, tennis racket, bed — though the bed is not a receptacle, it is an object for spatial reference)

3. Spatial relationships:
   - The laptop and lamp are on the dresser.
   - The tennis racket is on the floor, near the bed and partially under it.
   - The dresser is positioned against the wall and is adjacent to the bed area.
   - The robot is not currently at any object’s location (initial state).

4. Current state of each object relevant to the planning task:
   - The tennis racket is not held by any agent and is not in any receptacle (it is on the floor).
   - The dresser is not a receptacle, so the robot cannot use PutObjectInReceptacle on it. However, the instruction implies placing the racket “on” the dresser, which may require a different action not defined in the domain (e.g., “PutObjectOnSurface”).
   - No objects are currently being held by the agent.
   - No objects are clean, hot, cool, sliced, or toggled — these predicates are not relevant to the current task.
   - The laptop and lamp are stationary on the dresser.

5. Colors and visual properties:
   - Dresser: medium-brown wood with visible grain and metal drawer knobs.
   - Laptop: dark grey chassis, black screen, grey keyboard.
   - Lamp: white conical shade, dark blue cylindrical base.
   - Tennis racket: red-orange frame, yellow strings, green grip.
   - Bed: dark blue blanket, two green tufted pillows, dark grey frame.
   - Floor: light beige carpet with subtle texture.

Note: The domain does not include an action for placing an object on a non-receptacle surface like a dresser. The only “put” action defined is PutObjectInReceptacle, which requires a receptacle. Therefore, the instruction “Put a racket on a dresser” cannot be executed using the current domain actions unless the dresser is reclassified as a receptacle or a new action is added.

Based on the above scene caption, the images, and the instruction, generate the PDDL problem file.
Use the caption to understand what objects are present and their current state.
Make sure the PDDL problem accurately reflects the scene described in the caption.

Generate the PDDL problem:
attempts.response:
The provided domain file does not include an action for placing an object on a non-receptacle surface such as a dresser. The only “put” action available is `PutObjectInReceptacle`, which requires the target to be a receptacle (microwave, fridge, or sink). Since the dresser is not defined as a receptacle in the domain, and no action exists to place an object “on” a generic object like a dresser, the instruction “Put a racket on a dresser” cannot be expressed within the constraints of this domain.

However, to comply with the task and generate a PDDL problem that reflects the scene and instruction as closely as possible, we must make an assumption: either the dresser is treated as a receptacle (even though it is not in the domain’s type hierarchy), or we interpret “on” as “in” for the purpose of this problem. Since the domain does not allow extending types or adding new actions, we must work within its constraints.

Given the instruction and the domain, the only feasible interpretation is to treat the dresser as a receptacle for the sake of this problem — even though it is not listed in the domain’s receptacle types. This is a necessary workaround to make the problem solvable within the given domain.

Alternatively, if we strictly adhere to the domain, the goal cannot be achieved, and no valid PDDL problem can be generated. But since the task requires generating a PDDL problem, we proceed with the assumption that the dresser is a receptacle for this specific problem.

---

PDDL Problem:

```lisp
(define (problem put_racket_on_dresser)
    (:domain put_task)
    (:objects
        robot - agent
        dresser - receptacle
        tennis_racket - object
        laptop - object
        lamp - object
        bed - object
    )
    (:init
        (atLocation robot tennis_racket)
        (not (holdsAny robot))
        (not (inReceptacle tennis_racket dresser))
        (not (atLocation robot dresser))
        (openable dresser)
        (not (opened dresser))
        ; The laptop and lamp are on the dresser, but since dresser is not a receptacle in domain, we cannot model them as inReceptacle.
        ; For simplicity, we ignore them as they are not relevant to the goal.
    )
    (:goal (and
        (inReceptacle tennis_racket dresser)
    ))
)
```

---

**Note**: This solution assumes that `dresser` is a receptacle, which is not defined in the domain’s type hierarchy. In a real scenario, this would require modifying the domain to include `dresser` as a receptacle or adding a new action for placing objects on surfaces. Since the domain is fixed, this is the only way to generate a PDDL problem that matches the instruction.
attempts.problem:
(define (problem put_racket_on_dresser)
    (:domain put_task)
    (:objects
        robot - agent
        dresser - receptacle
        tennis_racket - object
        laptop - object
        lamp - object
        bed - object
    )
    (:init
        (atLocation robot tennis_racket)
        (not (holdsAny robot))
        (not (inReceptacle tennis_racket dresser))
        (not (atLocation robot dresser))
        (openable dresser)
        (not (opened dresser))
        ; The laptop and lamp are on the dresser, but since dresser is not a receptacle in domain, we cannot model them as inReceptacle.
        ; For simplicity, we ignore them as they are not relevant to the goal.
    )
    (:goal (and
        (inReceptacle tennis_racket dresser)
    ))
)
attempts.plan:
(pickupobject robot tennis_racket)
(gotolocation robot dresser)
(putobjectinreceptacle robot tennis_racket dresser)
